meta.name: Upper GI Bleed — Hematemesis and Melena with Hemorrhagic Shock

patient.name: Frank Delgado
patient.chiefComplaint: Multiple episodes of bright-red vomiting and black tarry stools, now weak and lightheaded

history.hpi: 68-year-old male with a history of alcohol use, cirrhosis, hypertension, and recent hospitalization for atrial fibrillation (now on apixaban) developed dyspepsia and dark stools over the past two days but didn't seek care. Tonight he had a sudden episode of vomiting bright-red blood with clots — initially he thought it was about a cup, then a second large episode 20 minutes later. His wife called 911 when he became pale, sweaty, and almost passed out trying to walk to the bathroom. He has been having melena (black tarry stools) for two days. No abdominal pain other than mild epigastric burning. No reported chest pain, headache, or trauma. He drinks 6-8 beers daily and stopped 'a few days ago' before his AFib admission. Last apixaban dose was this morning.
pastMedical: Cirrhosis (alcohol-related, esophageal varices documented on prior EGD)
Chronic alcohol use (6-8 beers/day, recently stopped)
medications: Apixaban 5 mg PO BID (last dose this morning)
history.lastOralIntake: Light dinner ~3 hours ago; vomited everything since
history.events: Two days of melena (black tarry stools), then sudden hematemesis tonight (~1 cup bright red blood with clots) followed by a second larger episode and near-syncope when standing.

scene.dispatch: Dispatch: 68-year-old male, vomiting blood, weak and almost passed out. Wife on scene.
scene.location: Single-family home, master bathroom and adjacent bedroom. Patient slumped on the bathroom floor next to the toilet.
scene.safety: Scene secure. Wife is upset but cooperative. Bathroom is small and crowded — plan extrication carefully. No environmental hazards but blood spatter is visible on multiple surfaces.
scene.bystanders: Wife (primary historian) reports two days of black, foul-smelling stools and onset of bright-red vomiting tonight. Confirms apixaban use, last dose this morning. Notes patient drinks 6-8 beers daily and stopped a few days ago. Reports cirrhosis and prior endoscopy with 'varices.'
visualCues: Patient slumped on the bathroom floor, leaning against the toilet, eyes half-open
Toilet bowl contains bright-red blood with clots and dark tarry stool
Pool of bright-red emesis on the bathroom floor (~500 mL visible)
Patient is markedly pale with cool, clammy skin
Multiple blood spatters on the bathroom mirror and tile
Empty apixaban bottle visible on bathroom counter with current fill date
Patient appears mildly jaundiced; spider angiomata visible on chest

phases.name: Initial Contact
phases.description: EMS finds a 68-year-old anticoagulated cirrhotic patient in hemorrhagic shock from a likely variceal bleed. He has had at least 2 episodes of large-volume hematemesis, ongoing melena for 2 days, and is now hypotensive, tachycardic, with cool clammy skin and altered mental status. This is the initial contact phase: dispatch, SAMPLE history, scene findings, and primary survey drive the response.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Patent for now but at high risk — patient just vomited large-volume blood, gag reflex sluggish, recurrent emesis likely
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Tachypneic, shallow respirations; clear bilateral lung sounds
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Weak, rapid radial pulse; cap refill 4 sec; cool clammy skin; no peripheral edema; no JVD

skin.color: marked pallor with mild scleral icterus

clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Moves all extremities slowly to commands; no focal deficit
otherFindings: Lethargic but rousable; gives one-word answers
Spider angiomata on chest, mild scleral icterus, palmar erythema
No abdominal distention or pulsatile mass; femoral pulses equal
Visible blood in mouth and dried blood on lips and chin
No signs of head trauma; no focal weakness
Has not urinated since the bleeding started
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: 'I'm so dizzy... I just couldn't stop... my head is spinning.'

expectedActions.shortAction: Recognize hemorrhagic shock (HR 132, BP 78/48, EtCO2 26, AMS, pallor)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Inclusion Criteria: Signs of poor perfusion (due to a medical cause) such as one or more of the following: Altered mental status; Delayed capillary refill (> 3 seconds); Decreased urine output; Respiratory rate greater than 20 breaths per minute in adults; Hypotension for age; Tachycardia; Weak, decreased or bounding pulses; Cool/mottled or flushed/ruddy skin.
expectedActions.rationale: Multiple shock criteria are met; patient needs immediate volume and minimum scene time.

expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Treatment and Interventions: Open and maintain patent airway. Suction airway: for significantly contaminated airways, consider utilizing a suction assisted laryngeal airway decontamination (SALAD) technique. Patient positioning can significantly impact respiratory mechanics... Elevating the head or padding (shoulders, occiput) can assist with opening airway and respiratory mechanics. This can both improve the ability to ventilate and limit aspiration.
expectedActions.rationale: Recurrent hematemesis with declining mental status = imminent aspiration; lateral positioning + ready suction is mandatory.

expectedActions.shortAction: High-flow O2 via NRB; titrate to 94-98%
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Treatment and Interventions: Administer oxygen as appropriate with a target of achieving 94–98% saturation.
expectedActions.rationale: Maximizes O2 delivery in profound anemia/shock state where SpO2 alone is misleading.

expectedActions.shortAction: Two large-bore IVs (16-18g); IO if no IV in 2 attempts
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Treatment and Interventions: Establish IV access. If unable to obtain within two attempts or less than 90 seconds, place an IO needle.
expectedActions.rationale: Hemorrhagic shock requires high-flow access and a redundant line.

expectedActions.shortAction: NS 500 mL bolus pressure-infused; reassess; up to 30 mL/kg (≈2.3 L)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Adult: Physiologic target: MAP goal 65 mmHg. Fluid goal of up to 30 mL/kg of isotonic fluid by administering rapid, predetermined boluses (e.g., 500 mL) unless the MAP goal is achieved, or pulmonary edema develops. If available, the administration of packed red blood cells or whole blood may be indicated for hemorrhagic shock.
expectedActions.rationale: Crystalloid as a bridge to blood products; titrate to MAP, watching for cirrhotic third-spacing.

expectedActions.shortAction: Request blood products if available; pre-arrival notification
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: If available, the administration of packed red blood cells or whole blood may be indicated for hemorrhagic shock.
expectedActions.rationale: Blood is the definitive resuscitation fluid in hemorrhagic shock; many systems have prehospital pRBC or whole blood programs.

expectedActions.shortAction: Cardiac monitor + 12-lead (afib known; rule out demand ischemia)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Treatment and Interventions: Cardiac monitor. EKG.
expectedActions.rationale: Severe anemia + tachycardia in elderly cardiac patient → demand ischemia is common and changes management.

expectedActions.shortAction: Place waveform EtCO2 (26 mmHg = poor perfusion)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Pulse oximetry and EtCO2 (reading of less than 25 mmHg may be sign of poor perfusion).
expectedActions.rationale: Tracks perfusion response to fluids in real time; very low EtCO2 = severe shock.

expectedActions.shortAction: Document anticoagulant (apixaban — last dose timing) for ED
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: History: History of GI bleeding; Cardiac problems.
expectedActions.rationale: Receiving facility needs anticoagulant identity and timing to consider andexanet alfa or 4-factor PCC.

expectedActions.shortAction: Rapid transport with pre-arrival notification (massive GI bleed, on apixaban)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Provide advanced notification to the hospital. Performance Measures: Percentage of patients with suspected shock for whom advanced notification to the hospital was provided.
expectedActions.rationale: Pre-arrival activation primes blood bank, GI consult, and ICU; reduces door-to-endoscopy time.

transitions.condition: Crew has placed two large-bore IVs, started rapid 500 mL boluses, applied O2, positioned for airway protection, and is en route with pre-arrival notification. Patient transiently improves: BP comes up to ~95 systolic, HR drops to 110s, mental status clearer.

Patient has a third large hematemesis episode during transport. Without lateral positioning and ready suction, aspiration occurs. SpO2 drops, oxygenation worsens, and crew must perform aggressive suction and airway support.

phases.name: Transient response
phases.description: After 1 L NS, patient is briefly more responsive, BP up to 96 systolic, HR down to 112. This is a transient response — classic Class III shock physiology. Crew continues rapid transport with serial reassessment, recognizing that crystalloid is a bridge, not a definitive fix in active hemorrhage.
phases.triggerCondition: Crew gave 1 L NS in 500 mL boluses, with transient improvement in vitals and mental status. Hemorrhage source is uncontrolled and ongoing — rebleeding is expected.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Patent; positioned in left lateral with suction at hand
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Stronger radial pulse, cap refill 3 sec; warming peripheries

skin.color: still pale but pinker than initial

clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Moves all extremities; following commands more reliably
otherFindings: Repeat hematemesis x1 small amount during transport (~50 mL)
Continues to bleed slowly per ongoing emesis
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: 'My stomach is still really upset. I feel like I'm going to throw up again.'

expectedActions.shortAction: Ondansetron 4 mg IV/SL (after 12-lead — afib + meds = QT risk)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Ondansetron (contraindicated for suspected or known diagnosis of prolonged QT syndrome). Adult: 4 mg IV/PO/SL.
expectedActions.rationale: Reduces vomiting recurrence and aspiration risk; verify QT first because of metoprolol + cirrhosis context.

expectedActions.shortAction: Reassess vitals q3-5min; expect transient response only
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Reassess vital signs and response to therapeutic interventions throughout transport. Key Documentation Elements: Full vital signs (pulse, blood pressure, respiratory rate, neurologic status assessment) with reassessment q 15 minutes or more frequently as appropriate.
expectedActions.rationale: Continued rebleeding is expected; frequent reassessment catches the recurrence early.

expectedActions.shortAction: Watch for rales/JVD (cirrhotic patient — third-spacing risk)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: Recognition of cardiogenic shock - If the patient condition deteriorates after fluid administration, rales or hepatomegaly develop, then consider cardiogenic shock and withholding further fluid administration.
expectedActions.rationale: Cirrhosis predisposes to fluid overload; do not over-resuscitate without blood product availability.

expectedActions.shortAction: Keep yankauer + battery suction ready and pre-tested
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Treatment and Interventions: Open and maintain patent airway. Suction airway: for significantly contaminated airways, consider utilizing a suction assisted laryngeal airway decontamination (SALAD) technique.
expectedActions.rationale: Recurrent hematemesis is anticipated; pre-staged suction prevents aspiration.

phases.name: Rebleed with aspiration
phases.description: During transport, patient has a third large hematemesis. Because the crew did not position laterally or have suction immediately available, aspiration occurs. SpO2 drops to the high 80s, the airway is contaminated with blood, and the crew now needs aggressive SALAD-style suction, BVM with PEEP, and possibly an advanced airway. This branch teaches the cost of skipping anticipatory airway preparation in massive GI bleed.
phases.triggerCondition: Crew did not position the patient to protect the airway and/or suction was not pre-staged. A third hematemesis event led to aspiration with hypoxia and worsening shock.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Heavily contaminated with blood and clots; gurgling on every breath; gag reflex obtunded
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Labored, gurgling respirations; coarse crackles bilaterally; cyanosis appearing
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Weak rapid pulse; cap refill 5 sec; cold extremities
otherFindings: Massive hematemesis just occurred — patient could not protect airway
Visible blood in trachea, audible gurgling
Mental status has dropped two GCS points

expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Treatment and Interventions: Suction airway: for significantly contaminated airways, consider utilizing a suction assisted laryngeal airway decontamination (SALAD) technique.
expectedActions.rationale: Recovery action — clearing the airway is mandatory before any positive-pressure ventilation in a contaminated airway.

expectedActions.shortAction: Place in lateral recovery position; head up if BP allows
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient positioning can significantly impact respiratory mechanics. Patients with severe bronchospasm should be left in the position of comfort (perhaps tripod) whenever possible. Elevating the head or padding (shoulders, occiput) can assist with opening airway and respiratory mechanics. This can both improve the ability to ventilate and limit aspiration.
expectedActions.rationale: Reduces ongoing aspiration during the rebleed.

expectedActions.shortAction: Two-person BVM with PEEP 5; tidal volume just to chest rise
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Use bag-valve-mask (BVM) ventilation in the setting of respiratory failure or arrest. Whenever possible, the patient's head should be elevated up to 30 degrees. Two-person, two-thumbs-up BVM ventilation is preferred. PEEP should be used with BVM. 5 cmH2O is generally an appropriate initial PEEP setting.
expectedActions.rationale: PEEP recruits collapsed alveoli post-aspiration; two-person technique improves seal in a soiled airway.

expectedActions.shortAction: Consider SGA or intubation if BVM fails to oxygenate
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Supraglottic airways (SGA): Consider the use of an appropriately sized SGA if BVM (with OPA/NPA) alone is not effective in maintaining oxygenation and/or ventilation. Endotracheal intubation: When less-invasive methods (two-person BVM, SGA placement) are ineffective or inappropriate, consider endotracheal intubation to maintain oxygenation and/or ventilation.
expectedActions.rationale: Severe aspiration with hypoxia despite BVM is an indication to escalate; SGA before intubation per protocol.

expectedActions.shortAction: Emergent transport — ED prep for massive transfusion
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Provide advanced notification to the hospital.
expectedActions.rationale: This is a dual-hit (hemorrhage + aspiration); receiving facility needs to mobilize MTP and airway team.
